turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having some pretty bad crampy pains in my lower left abdomen, and I've also noticed that I'm going to the bathroom way more than usual - like 8 or 10 times a day. It's been really uncomfortable and it's affecting my work, so I wanted to see if there's something going on that needs attention.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's hard to describe, but the pain is crampy and feels like it's tightening up my abdomen. Sometimes it's sharp, other times it's just a dull ache, but it's always in the lower left side. And with the diarrhea, I feel like I'm losing control - I'll be at work or on my way to a meeting and suddenly I need to go really badly. It's embarrassing and frustrating, especially since I've been using Imodium for two weeks now and it hasn't helped much.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: To be honest, when the pain is bad and the diarrhea hits, I feel really wiped out. It's like all my energy just disappears, and I get this overwhelming fatigue that makes it hard for me to concentrate or even make it through a meeting without wanting to go back home and take a nap.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: No, I don't really notice any sweating or feeling clammy. It's mostly just the crampy pain, diarrhea, and fatigue that I'm dealing with.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, there's no sweating or feeling clammy.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: It seems to stay mostly on the lower left side of my abdomen, but sometimes it can radiate up a bit towards my back. The pain is usually most intense when I'm trying to have a bowel movement, and after that, it might ease up for a little while before coming back again.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: No, it mostly stays around my lower abdomen and sometimes goes into my back. I don't feel any pain in my arms, neck, or jaw.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, there's no tingling or numbness in my arms. The discomfort is mostly localized to my lower abdomen and back, like I mentioned before.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, I don't feel anything unusual in my legs or feet. It's all pretty much limited to my lower abdomen and back.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: No, there's no swelling at all - I've checked my ankles and feet a few times because I'm experiencing this achy pain in both knees and ankles, but they look normal. The pain is just kind of... lingering, you know? It's not going away.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: No, my shoes don't feel tighter and I don't notice any puffiness in my legs. To be honest, it's more of a dull ache that's been going on for a while now, especially in my knees and ankles.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: Honestly, yeah. The diarrhea is pretty loose and watery, and it feels like I'm going to vomit sometimes when I'm having a bad episode. But so far, I haven't actually thrown up. It's more of a feeling of impending nausea that passes once the diarrhea does.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yeah, I do get a bit queasy and it feels like my stomach is upset when the diarrhea hits. It's not just the pain and discomfort, but there's also this feeling of nausea that comes with it.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: Yes, I've noticed a low-grade fever a few times per week. It's not super high, usually around 37.8 to 38 degrees Celsius.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: To be honest, my appetite hasn't been great lately, but I think that's partly because of the stress and long hours at work, not just because of the symptoms. As for weight loss, yeah - I've lost about 4 kilograms over the past three weeks, which is concerning me a bit.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient: I've been trying to stick to regular meals, but it's hard with my work schedule. Mostly takeout and fast food, not much time for cooking at home. I do try to get some exercise in, usually a 30-minute jog or bike ride during lunch breaks, but it's been inconsistent lately due to the fatigue from the symptoms. As for daily routine, I've been working over 60 hours a week, which is taking a toll on my sleep and overall well-being.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: To be honest, physical activity has been really tough for me lately. I used to enjoy jogging and biking during lunch breaks, but with this fatigue from the diarrhea and crampy pains, it's hard to find the energy for even a 30-minute workout. Some days I just can't make it through my usual routine without feeling exhausted.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my diet has been pretty irregular due to work hours and stress. It's mostly takeout and fast food - I'll grab something on the go or order in when I'm too tired to cook at home. Some days I might skip meals altogether because I just don't have time. I know it's not ideal, but with my schedule, it's hard to plan healthy meals and stick to a routine.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30. She had some similar symptoms, like abdominal pain and diarrhea, before her diagnosis, but it wasn't until later that they confirmed she had Crohn's. I've been a bit worried about my own situation because of her experience, to be honest.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. It's been a bit of a concern for me, especially given my own symptoms and what I've learned about her experience.
